name: TLSF
summary: Two-Level Segregated Fit allocator delivering bounded-time *O(1)* malloc/free for hard real-time systems via two bitmap-indexed levels of segregated free lists, located in O(1) using `clz`/`ffs`.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Two-Level Segregated Fit (TLSF) is the textbook real-time allocator. It delivers bounded-time *O(1)* malloc/free for hard real-time systems via two bitmap-indexed levels of segregated free lists, located in O(1) using count-leading-zeros / find-first-set instructions. It is the only allocator in the chapter giving a hard worst-case bound — the right choice when latency determinism rather than throughput is the goal.

Unlike the throughput-oriented (mimalloc, snmalloc, rpmalloc) and fleet-scale (TCMalloc, jemalloc) families, TLSF's single quality-of-interest is *predictability*: every allocation must complete in a fixed number of machine instructions regardless of heap size or fragmentation state.

sections.title: Two-level bitmap and O(1) fit
sections.content:
First-level bitmap partitions the size space by power-of-two; second-level bitmap subdivides each first-level bucket into linear ranges. A request is rounded up to the smallest fitting size class; a `clz`/`ffs` over the first-level mask finds the smallest non-empty power-of-two bucket large enough, a second `ffs` finds the specific bin within. Two bit-scan instructions locate a fitting block regardless of how many distinct sizes the heap currently holds.

Metadata cost is roughly 4 bytes per allocation; reported fragmentation is typically below 15%, with a worst-case bound below 25%. Pools can be added or removed on the fly, and `TLSF-EXT` supports externalised headers for non-memory resource arenas (file descriptors, GPU buffers).

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content: Production: Eclipse 4diac FORTE IEC 61499 runtime, Linux distros (in-kernel and userland tools), Ravenscar / hard-RT firmware, multimedia codecs, network appliances. The bounded-time guarantee is what makes TLSF appropriate when an allocation latency spike could cause a missed deadline — industrial control, avionics-adjacent firmware, and packet-processing fast paths are the canonical fit.
